result:
At 4:17 a.m., the city belongs to machines and birds.

Traffic lights keep changing for no one, serious and disciplined as monks. Refrigerators hum behind apartment walls. A bakery on the corner exhales warm air that smells like memory before anyone is awake enough to call it that. Somewhere, a crow gives one test caw, like tapping a microphone before a speech.

This is my favorite imaginary hour—when everything still works, but nothing is performing. No one is curating themselves. No one is trying to be impressive. Even the sky is undecided, charcoal at the edges, blue in the middle, like a thought not fully formed.

I think people underestimate how often life is made of transitions.

Not arrivals. Not declarations. Not the dramatic “before” and “after” moments that biographies like to circle in red marker. Mostly it’s thresholds: hand on the doorknob, cursor blinking, kettle warming, elevator descending, breath in, breath out. We spend most of our existence becoming, not being.

Maybe that’s why dawn feels honest. It doesn’t pretend to be finished.

By 8:43 a.m., the city will have opinions again. Coffee orders, inboxes, construction drills, weather complaints, polished small talk in office kitchens. But for this little sliver of time, the world feels like an instrument waiting to be played. And maybe that’s enough—a reminder that before the noise, there is potential; before the narrative, there is presence; before the day asks us to explain ourselves, we are simply here.

Sometimes I think peace isn’t a place you go.
It’s a pace you choose.
